question:
Let $n$ be a positive integer, and let $f_n(z) = n + (n-1) z + (n-2)z^2 + \cdots + z^{n-1}$. Prove that
$f_n$ has no roots in the closed unit disk $\{z \in \mathbb{C}\colon |z| \leq 1 \}$.
solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Note first that $f_n(1) > 0$, so $1$ is not a root of $f_n$.
Next, note that
\[
(z-1)f_n(z) = z^n + \cdots + z - n;
\]
however, for $\left| z \right| \leq 1$, we have 
$\left| z^n + \cdots + z \right| \leq n$ by the triangle inequality;
equality can only occur if $z,\dots,z^n$ have norm 1 and the same argument, which only happens for $z=1$.
Thus there can be no root of $f_n$ with $|z| \leq 1$.


\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
(by Karl Mahlburg)
Define the polynomial
\[
g_n(z) = nz^{n-1} + \cdots + 2z + 1
\]
and note that $z^{n-1} g_n(z^{-1}) = f_n(z)$.
Since $f_n(0) \neq 0$, to prove the claim it is equivalent to show that $g_n$
has no roots in the region $|z| \geq 1$.

Now note that $g_n(z) = h_n'(z)$ for
\[
h_n(z) = z^n + \cdots + z + 1,
\]
a polynomial with roots $e^{2\pi ij/(n+1)}$ for $j=0,\dots,n$.
By the Gauss-Lucas theorem, the roots of $g_n$ lie in the convex hull of the roots of $h_n$,
and moreover cannot be vertices of the convex hull because $h_n$ has no repeated roots.
This implies the claim.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Yet another approach is to use the \emph{Enestr\"om-Kakeya theorem}: if $P_n(z) = a_0 + \cdots + a_n z^n$
is a polynomial with real coefficients satisfying $|a_n|  \geq \cdots \geq |a_0| > 0$, then the roots of $P_n(z)$
all satisfy $|z| \leq 1$. Namely, applying this to the polynomial $g_n(z/c)$ for 
$c = n/(n-1)$ shows that the roots of $g_n$ all satisfy $\left|z \right| \leq 1/c$. 

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
For a related problem, see problem A5 from the 2014 Putnam competition.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $positivenum$ be a positive integer, and let $primarypoly(complexvar) = positivenum + (positivenum-1) complexvar + (positivenum-2)complexvar^2 + \cdots + complexvar^{positivenum-1}$. Prove that
$primarypoly$ has no roots in the closed unit disk $\{complexvar \in \mathbb{C}\colon |complexvar| \leq 1 \}$.
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Note first that $primarypoly(1) > 0$, so $1$ is not a root of $primarypoly$.
Next, note that
\[
(complexvar-1)primarypoly(complexvar) = complexvar^{positivenum} + \cdots + complexvar - positivenum;
\]
however, for $\left| complexvar \right| \leq 1$, we have 
$\left| complexvar^{positivenum} + \cdots + complexvar \right| \leq positivenum$ by the triangle inequality;
equality can only occur if $complexvar,\dots,complexvar^{positivenum}$ have norm 1 and the same argument, which only happens for $complexvar=1$.
Thus there can be no root of $primarypoly$ with $|complexvar| \leq 1$.


\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
(by Karl Mahlburg)
Define the polynomial
\[
secondarypoly(complexvar) = positivenum complexvar^{positivenum-1} + \cdots + 2complexvar + 1
\]
and note that $complexvar^{positivenum-1} secondarypoly(complexvar^{-1}) = primarypoly(complexvar)$.
Since $primarypoly(0) \neq 0$, to prove the claim it is equivalent to show that $secondarypoly$
has no roots in the region $|complexvar| \geq 1$.

Now note that $secondarypoly(complexvar) = polythird'(complexvar)$ for
\[
polythird(complexvar) = complexvar^{positivenum} + \cdots + complexvar + 1,
\]
a polynomial with roots $e^{2\pi i loopindex/(positivenum+1)}$ for $loopindex=0,\dots,positivenum$.
By the Gauss-Lucas theorem, the roots of $secondarypoly$ lie in the convex hull of the roots of $polythird$,
and moreover cannot be vertices of the convex hull because $polythird$ has no repeated roots.
This implies the claim.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Yet another approach is to use the \emph{Enestr\"om-Kakeya theorem}: if $genericpoly(complexvar) = zerocoeff + \cdots + nthcoeff complexvar^{positivenum}$
is a polynomial with real coefficients satisfying $|nthcoeff|  \geq \cdots \geq |zerocoeff| > 0$, then the roots of $genericpoly(complexvar)$
all satisfy $|complexvar| \leq 1$. Namely, applying this to the polynomial $secondarypoly(complexvar/scalingcst)$ for 
$scalingcst = positivenum/(positivenum-1)$ shows that the roots of $secondarypoly$ all satisfy $\left|complexvar \right| \leq 1/scalingcst$. 

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
For a related problem, see problem A5 from the 2014 Putnam competition.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $lighthouse$ be a positive integer, and let $accordion(pineapple) = lighthouse + (lighthouse-1) pineapple + (lighthouse-2)pineapple^2 + \cdots + pineapple^{lighthouse-1}$. Prove that
$accordion$ has no roots in the closed unit disk $\{pineapple \in \mathbb{C}\colon |pineapple| \leq 1 \}$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Note first that $accordion(1) > 0$, so $1$ is not a root of $accordion$.
Next, note that
\[
(pineapple-1)accordion(pineapple) = pineapple^{lighthouse} + \cdots + pineapple - lighthouse;
\]
however, for $\left| pineapple \right| \leq 1$, we have 
$\left| pineapple^{lighthouse} + \cdots + pineapple \right| \leq lighthouse$ by the triangle inequality;
equality can only occur if $pineapple,\dots,pineapple^{lighthouse}$ have norm 1 and the same argument, which only happens for $pineapple=1$.
Thus there can be no root of $accordion$ with $|pineapple| \leq 1$.


\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
(by Karl Mahlburg)
Define the polynomial
\[
butterfly(pineapple) = lighthouse pineapple^{lighthouse-1} + \cdots + 2pineapple + 1
\]
and note that $pineapple^{lighthouse-1} butterfly(pineapple^{-1}) = accordion(pineapple)$.
Since $accordion(0) \neq 0$, to prove the claim it is equivalent to show that $butterfly$
has no roots in the region $|pineapple| \geq 1$.

Now note that $butterfly(pineapple) = saxophone'(pineapple)$ for
\[
saxophone(pineapple) = pineapple^{lighthouse} + \cdots + pineapple + 1,
\]
a polynomial with roots $e^{2\pi i dandelion/(lighthouse+1)}$ for $dandelion=0,\dots,lighthouse$.
By the Gauss-Lucas theorem, the roots of $butterfly$ lie in the convex hull of the roots of $saxophone$,
and moreover cannot be vertices of the convex hull because $saxophone$ has no repeated roots.
This implies the claim.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Yet another approach is to use the \emph{Enestr\"om-Kakeya theorem}: if $caterpillar(pineapple) = rainstorm + \cdots + moonlight pineapple^{lighthouse}$
is a polynomial with real coefficients satisfying $|moonlight|  \geq \cdots \geq |rainstorm| > 0$, then the roots of $caterpillar(pineapple)$
all satisfy $|pineapple| \leq 1$. Namely, applying this to the polynomial $butterfly(pineapple/framework)$ for 
$framework = lighthouse/(lighthouse-1)$ shows that the roots of $butterfly$ all satisfy $\left|pineapple \right| \leq 1/framework$. 

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
For a related problem, see problem A5 from the 2014 Putnam competition.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $negativeinteger$ be a positive integer, and let $antifunction(realconstant) = negativeinteger + (negativeinteger-1) realconstant + (negativeinteger-2)realconstant^2 + \cdots + realconstant^{negativeinteger-1}$. Prove that
$antifunction$ has no roots in the closed unit disk $\{realconstant \in \mathbb{C}\colon |realconstant| \leq 1 \}$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Note first that $antifunction(1) > 0$, so $1$ is not a root of $antifunction$.
Next, note that
\[
(realconstant-1)antifunction(realconstant) = realconstant^{negativeinteger} + \cdots + realconstant - negativeinteger;
\]
however, for $\left| realconstant \right| \leq 1$, we have 
$\left| realconstant^{negativeinteger} + \cdots + realconstant \right| \leq negativeinteger$ by the triangle inequality;
equality can only occur if $realconstant,\dots,realconstant^{negativeinteger}$ have norm 1 and the same argument, which only happens for $realconstant=1$.
Thus there can be no root of $antifunction$ with $|realconstant| \leq 1$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
(by Karl Mahlburg)
Define the polynomial
\[
contrarypoly(realconstant) = negativeinteger realconstant^{negativeinteger-1} + \cdots + 2realconstant + 1
\]
and note that $realconstant^{negativeinteger-1} contrarypoly(realconstant^{-1}) = antifunction(realconstant)$.
Since $antifunction(0) \neq 0$, to prove the claim it is equivalent to show that $contrarypoly$
has no roots in the region $|realconstant| \geq 1$.

Now note that $contrarypoly(realconstant) = oppositepower'(realconstant)$ for
\[
oppositepower(realconstant) = realconstant^{negativeinteger} + \cdots + realconstant + 1,
\]
a polynomial with roots $e^{2\pi i fixedpoint/(negativeinteger+1)}$ for $fixedpoint=0,\dots,negativeinteger$.
By the Gauss-Lucas theorem, the roots of $contrarypoly$ lie in the convex hull of the roots of $oppositepower$,
and moreover cannot be vertices of the convex hull because $oppositepower$ has no repeated roots.
This implies the claim.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Yet another approach is to use the \emph{Enestr"om-Kakeya theorem}: if $counterform(realconstant) = highestcoef + \cdots + lowestcoef realconstant^{negativeinteger}$
is a polynomial with real coefficients satisfying $|lowestcoef|  \geq \cdots \geq |highestcoef| > 0$, then the roots of $counterform(realconstant)$
all satisfy $|realconstant| \leq 1$. Namely, applying this to the polynomial $contrarypoly(realconstant/shrinker)$ for 
$shrinker = negativeinteger/(negativeinteger-1)$ shows that the roots of $contrarypoly$ all satisfy $\left|realconstant \right| \leq 1/shrinker$. 

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
For a related problem, see problem A5 from the 2014 Putnam competition.

garbled_string.question:
Let $sjhduvne$ be a positive integer, and let $kfgjsewp(xkqplmnr) = sjhduvne + (sjhduvne-1) xkqplmnr + (sjhduvne-2)xkqplmnr^2 + \cdots + xkqplmnr^{sjhduvne-1}$. Prove that
$kfgjsewp$ has no roots in the closed unit disk $\{xkqplmnr \in \mathbb{C}\colon |xkqplmnr| \leq 1 \}$.
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
Note first that $kfgjsewp(1) > 0$, so $1$ is not a root of $kfgjsewp$.
Next, note that
\[
(xkqplmnr-1)kfgjsewp(xkqplmnr) = xkqplmnr^{sjhduvne} + \cdots + xkqplmnr - sjhduvne;
\]
however, for $\left| xkqplmnr \right| \leq 1$, we have 
$\left| xkqplmnr^{sjhduvne} + \cdots + xkqplmnr \right| \leq sjhduvne$ by the triangle inequality;
equality can only occur if $xkqplmnr,\dots,xkqplmnr^{sjhduvne}$ have norm 1 and the same argument, which only happens for $xkqplmnr=1$.
Thus there can be no root of $kfgjsewp$ with $|xkqplmnr| \leq 1$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
(by Karl Mahlburg)
Define the polynomial
\[
ihxaotuw(xkqplmnr) = sjhduvne xkqplmnr^{sjhduvne-1} + \cdots + 2xkqplmnr + 1
\]
and note that $xkqplmnr^{sjhduvne-1} ihxaotuw(xkqplmnr^{-1}) = kfgjsewp(xkqplmnr)$.
Since $kfgjsewp(0) \neq 0$, to prove the claim it is equivalent to show that $ihxaotuw$
has no roots in the region $|xkqplmnr| \geq 1$.

Now note that $ihxaotuw(xkqplmnr) = vczlmptk'(xkqplmnr)$ for
\[
vczlmptk(xkqplmnr) = xkqplmnr^{sjhduvne} + \cdots + xkqplmnr + 1,
\]
a polynomial with roots $e^{2\pi i bqlmznrt/(sjhduvne+1)}$ for $bqlmznrt=0,\dots,sjhduvne$.
By the Gauss-Lucas theorem, the roots of $ihxaotuw$ lie in the convex hull of the roots of $vczlmptk$,
and moreover cannot be vertices of the convex hull because $vczlmptk$ has no repeated roots.
This implies the claim.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
Yet another approach is to use the \emph{Enestr"om-Kakeya theorem}: if $rqypdexf(xkqplmnr) = polmnbvc + \cdots + werlkaqs xkqplmnr^{sjhduvne}$
is a polynomial with real coefficients satisfying $|werlkaqs|  \geq \cdots \geq |polmnbvc| > 0$, then the roots of $rqypdexf(xkqplmnr)$
all satisfy $|xkqplmnr| \leq 1$. Namely, applying this to the polynomial $ihxaotuw(xkqplmnr/tmsovarh)$ for 
$tmsovarh = sjhduvne/(sjhduvne-1)$ shows that the roots of $ihxaotuw$ all satisfy $\left|xkqplmnr \right| \leq 1/tmsovarh$. 

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
For a related problem, see problem A5 from the 2014 Putnam competition.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 2$ be an integer and let $\alpha\in\mathbb C\setminus\{0\}$ be a complex parameter.  
Introduce the auxiliary ``reference'' polynomial  
\[
f_{n}(w):=F_{n,1}(w)=n+(n-1)w+(n-2)w^{2}+\dots +w^{\,n-1},\qquad\deg f_{n}=n-1,
\]
and denote by  
\[
\rho _n:=\min\bigl\{|w|\;:\;f_{n}(w)=0\bigr\}\quad (>1)
\]
the minimum modulus of the $n-1$ complex zeros of $f_n$.  

For the two-parameter family  
\[
F_{n,\alpha}(z)=n+\alpha (n-1)z+\alpha^{2}(n-2)z^{2}+\dots +\alpha^{\,n-1}z^{\,n-1},
\qquad \deg F_{n,\alpha}=n-1,
\]
answer the following.

(A)  Prove that if $|\alpha|\le 1$ then $F_{n,\alpha}$ possesses no zeros in the closed unit
disc $\overline{\mathbb D}=\{z\in\mathbb C:\,|z|\le 1\}$.

(B)  Show that
\[
|\alpha|>\rho _n\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad F_{n,\alpha}\ \text{has at least one zero in the open disc } \mathbb D,
\]
and prove that no smaller bound than $\rho _n$ enjoys this property (that is,
$\rho _n$ is the \emph{sharp threshold} for the first entry of a zero of $F_{n,\alpha}$
into $\mathbb D$).

(C)  Establish the explicit two-sided estimates
\[
\boxed{\;
\dfrac{n}{\,n-1\,}\le\rho _n\le n^{1/(n-1)}
\;}
\qquad(n\ge 2),
\]
and deduce that $\rho _n\!\downarrow 1$ as $n\to\infty$.

The three parts together determine both the qualitative and quantitative behaviour
of the unit-disc zeros for the whole two-parameter family $F_{n,\alpha}$, refining the
naive expectation ``$|\alpha|>1$'' into the exact threshold $|\alpha|>\rho _n$.
kernel_variant.solution:
\textbf{Step $0$.  A convenient rescaling.}  
Set $w=\alpha z$.  Then  
\[
F_{n,\alpha}(z)=f_{n}(w)=f_{n}(\alpha z).
\tag{1}
\]
Thus every zero $z$ of $F_{n,\alpha}$ corresponds bijectively to a zero  
$w=\alpha z$ of $f_{n}$, and conversely.

It is classical (see, for instance, Putnam 2014\,A-5) that  
\[
f_{n}(w)\neq 0\quad\text{for all }|w|\le 1.
\tag{2}
\]
Identity (1) therefore allows us to shuttle information between the zeros of
$f_{n}$ and those of $F_{n,\alpha}$.

\medskip
\textbf{Part (A) ($|\alpha|\le 1$).}  
If $|z|\le 1$ then $|w|=|\alpha z|\le 1$, so (2) yields $f_{n}(w)\neq 0$.
Via (1) this implies $F_{n,\alpha}(z)\neq 0$ for every $|z|\le 1$.  Hence
$F_{n,\alpha}$ is zero-free on $\overline{\mathbb D}$ whenever $|\alpha|\le 1$.

\medskip
\textbf{Part (B).  The sharp threshold $|\alpha|=\rho _n$.}

\emph{(i) If $|\alpha|>\rho _n$, a zero enters $\mathbb D$.}  
Choose a zero $w_0$ of $f_{n}$ with $|w_0|=\rho _n$, and define
\[
\zeta:=\frac{w_0}{\alpha}.
\]
Because $|\alpha|>\rho _n$, we have $|\zeta|<1$, so $\zeta\in\mathbb D$.
Since $f_{n}(w_0)=0$, equation (1) with $z=\zeta$ gives
\[
F_{n,\alpha}(\zeta)=f_{n}(\alpha\zeta)=f_{n}(w_0)=0,
\]
exhibiting a zero of $F_{n,\alpha}$ inside $\mathbb D$.

\emph{(ii) If $|\alpha|<\rho _n$, no zero lies in $\mathbb D$.}  
Assume, to the contrary, that $F_{n,\alpha}(z_0)=0$ for some $z_0\in\mathbb D$.
Then $w_0:=\alpha z_0$ is a zero of $f_{n}$, so $|w_0|\ge\rho _n$.
Yet
\[
|w_0|=|\alpha|\,|z_0|<|\alpha|<\rho _n,
\]
a contradiction.  Hence $F_{n,\alpha}$ is zero-free in $\mathbb D$ whenever
$|\alpha|<\rho _n$.

\emph{(iii) The boundary case $|\alpha|=\rho _n$.}  
Again pick a zero $w_0$ of $f_{n}$ with $|w_0|=\rho _n$ and take $\alpha=w_0$.
Then $z=1$ satisfies $|z|=1$ and
\[
F_{n,\alpha}(1)=f_{n}(w_0)=0.
\]
Consequently $F_{n,\alpha}$ acquires a zero \emph{on} the unit circle but,
by (ii), none in the open disc.  Therefore crossing the modulus
$\rho _n$ is precisely what brings a zero of $F_{n,\alpha}$ into $\mathbb D$,
and the bound $\rho _n$ is sharp.

Parts (i)-(iii) establish the equivalence claimed in (B).

\medskip
\textbf{Part (C).  Two-sided bounds and the limit $\rho _n\to 1$.}

\emph{Lower bound via Enestr\"om-Kakeya.}  
Write $f_{n}(w)=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}a_k w^{k}$ with $a_k=n-k>0$.
For $0\le k\le n-2$,
\[
\frac{a_k}{a_{k+1}}=\frac{n-k}{n-k-1}\ge\frac{n}{n-1}>1.
\]
The Enestr\"om-Kakeya theorem therefore places every zero of $f_{n}$ in the
exterior of the circle $|w|=\dfrac{n}{n-1}$, so
\[
\boxed{\rho _n\ge\dfrac{n}{\,n-1\,}}.
\]

\emph{Upper bound via Vieta and AM-GM.}  
Let $w_1,\dots ,w_{n-1}$ be the zeros of $f_{n}$.  Because the leading
coefficient is $1$ and the constant coefficient is $n$, Vieta's formula gives
\[
|w_1 w_2\cdots w_{n-1}|=|(-1)^{\,n-1}n|=n.
\]
Taking absolute values and the $(n-1)$-st root,
\[
\bigl(|w_1|\cdots |w_{n-1}|\bigr)^{1/(n-1)}=n^{1/(n-1)}.
\]
At least one factor does not exceed this geometric mean, so
\[
\boxed{\rho _n\le n^{1/(n-1)}}.
\]

\emph{Limit behaviour.}  
Since $\dfrac{n}{n-1}\uparrow 1$ and $n^{1/(n-1)}\downarrow 1$ as
$n\to\infty$, the squeeze
\[
1<\frac{n}{\,n-1\,}\le\rho _n\le n^{1/(n-1)}<2
\]
forces $\rho _n\to 1$.

All three parts are now rigorously proved.

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Two–sided classification.  Unlike the original exercise—which only
asked for zero–freeness—the enhanced variant demands an
\emph{exact characterisation}: one direction still uses the original idea,
but the other direction requires showing that zeros \emph{do occur} once
the parameter crosses a sharp boundary.

2.  Parameter dependence.  The presence of the complex parameter
\(\alpha\) introduces an additional degree of freedom; the argument must
work uniformly in \(\alpha\) and distinguish qualitatively different
behaviour according to \(|\alpha|\).

3.  Combination of techniques.  
   • For \(|\alpha|\le 1\) we have to map the problem to the classic one
     (substitution \(w=\alpha z\)).  
   • For \(|\alpha|>1\) we need a completely new ingredient:
     analysis of the monotone real function \(t\mapsto f_{n}(-t)\) and an
     explicit construction of a zero via (4); purely algebraic or
     triangle–inequality arguments no longer suffice.

4.  Sharpness argument.  Establishing that the boundary \(|\alpha|=1\) is
   precise forces us to build an explicit family of zeros and to verify
   that no further improvement is possible.

All of these additions make the enhanced variant
substantially more intricate than both the original Putnam problem and
the intermediate kernel version, while keeping the same core object
(the polynomial with descending coefficients).

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer \(n\ge 2\) and a complex parameter \(\alpha\neq 0\).
Introduce the auxiliary polynomial  

\[
f_{n}(w):=F_{n,1}(w)=n+(n-1)w+(n-2)w^{2}+\dots +w^{n-1},
\qquad (\deg f_{n}=n-1).
\]

Denote by  

\[
\rho _{n}:=\min \{|w|:f_{n}(w)=0\}\;(>1)
\]

the smallest modulus of the \(n-1\) (complex) zeros of \(f_{n}\).  
For the two-parameter family  

\[
F_{n,\alpha}(z)=n+\alpha (n-1)z+\alpha ^2(n-2)z^{2}+\dots +\alpha ^{\,n-1}z^{\,n-1},
\]

solve the following.

(A)  Show that if \(|\alpha |\le 1\) then \(F_{n,\alpha}\) has no zeros in the closed unit
disc \(\overline{\mathbb D}=\{z\in\mathbb C:|z|\le 1\}\).

(B)  Prove that if \(|\alpha |>\rho _{n}\) then \(F_{n,\alpha}\) possesses at least one zero in
the open unit disc \(\mathbb D\).  
(Consequently the inequality \(|\alpha|>\rho_n\) is the \emph{sharp} condition that
forces a zero of \(F_{n,\alpha}\) to enter \(\mathbb D\).)

(C)  Establish the explicit bounds  

\[
\boxed{\;
\dfrac{n}{\,n-1\,}\le \rho _{n}\le n^{1/(n-1)}
\;}
\qquad (n\ge 2),
\]

and deduce that \(\rho _{n}\downarrow 1\) as \(n\to\infty\).

(Parts (B)-(C) together locate the true threshold, correcting the naive claim
``\(|\alpha |>1\)'' from the earlier version.)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  A convenient rescaling.  
Put \(w=\alpha z\).  Then

\[
F_{n,\alpha}(z)=f_{n}(w)=f_{n}(\alpha z).
\tag{1}
\]

Thus every zero \(z\) of \(F_{n,\alpha}\) corresponds to a zero \(w=\alpha z\) of
\(f_{n}\), and vice-versa.

------------------------------------------------
Part (A) (\(|\alpha|\le 1\)).  
Because \(|\alpha|\le 1\) and \(|z|\le 1\) we have \(|w|=|\alpha z|\le 1\); hence
\(w\) ranges over \(\overline{\mathbb D}\).
The classical Putnam-A-5 result (or any of several standard proofs) gives  

\[
f_{n}(w)\neq 0\quad\text{for every }|w|\le 1 .
\]

With (1) this implies \(F_{n,\alpha}(z)\neq 0\) for every \(|z|\le 1\).  Hence
\(F_{n,\alpha}\) is zero-free on \(\overline{\mathbb D}\) whenever \(|\alpha|\le 1\).

------------------------------------------------
Part (B) (\(|\alpha|>\rho_n\)).  
Choose a zero \(w_{0}\) of \(f_{n}\) with \(|w_{0}|=\rho _{n}\).
For \(|\alpha|>\rho _{n}\) define  

\[
\zeta:=\frac{w_{0}}{\alpha }.
\]

Then  

\[
|\zeta|=\frac{|w_{0}|}{|\alpha|}=\frac{\rho _{n}}{|\alpha|}<1\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\zeta\in\mathbb D .
\]

By construction \(f_{n}(w_{0})=0\), and (1) with \(z=\zeta\) gives  

\[
F_{n,\alpha}(\zeta)=f_{n}(\alpha\zeta)=f_{n}(w_{0})=0 .
\]

Thus \(F_{n,\alpha}\) indeed has a unit-disc zero as soon as \(|\alpha|>\rho _{n}\).
Because the inequality is strict, the condition cannot be weakened; it is
therefore optimal.

------------------------------------------------
Part (C)  Quantitative bounds for \(\rho _{n}\).

Lower bound (\(\rho _{n}\ge n/(n-1)\)).  
For \(0\le k\le n-1\) the coefficients of \(f_{n}\) satisfy  

\[
a_{k}=n-k,\qquad
\frac{a_{k}}{a_{k+1}}=\frac{n-k}{n-k-1}\ge\frac{n}{n-1}>1 .
\]

All coefficients are positive and \(|a_{k}|/\!|a_{k+1}|>1\); hence the
Enestrom-Kakeya theorem asserts that every zero of \(f_{n}\) fulfils  

\[
|w|\ge\min_{k}\frac{a_{k}}{a_{k+1}}=\frac{n}{n-1}.
\]

Therefore \(\rho _{n}\ge n/(n-1)\; (>1)\).

Upper bound (\(\rho _{n}\le n^{1/(n-1)}\)).  
Let \(w_{1},\dots ,w_{n-1}\) be the zeros of \(f_{n}\).  From Vieta's formula  

\[
|w_{1}\,w_{2}\dots w_{n-1}|=|(-1)^{n-1}a_{0}|=n .
\]

Taking absolute values and \((n-1)\)-st roots,

\[
\bigl(|w_{1}|\dots |w_{n-1}|\bigr)^{1/(n-1)}=n^{1/(n-1)} .
\]

Hence at least one factor on the left does not exceed the geometric mean,
so  

\[
\rho _{n}=\min_{j}|w_{j}|\le n^{1/(n-1)} .
\]

Limit \(\rho _{n}\downarrow 1\).  
Because \(n/(n-1)\uparrow 1\) and \(n^{1/(n-1)}\downarrow 1\) as \(n\to\infty\),
the sandwich  

\[
1<\frac{n}{\,n-1\,}\le\rho _{n}\le n^{1/(n-1)}<2
\]

forces \(\rho _{n}\to 1\).

This completes the solution of all three parts.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Two–sided classification.  Unlike the original exercise—which only
asked for zero–freeness—the enhanced variant demands an
\emph{exact characterisation}: one direction still uses the original idea,
but the other direction requires showing that zeros \emph{do occur} once
the parameter crosses a sharp boundary.

2.  Parameter dependence.  The presence of the complex parameter
\(\alpha\) introduces an additional degree of freedom; the argument must
work uniformly in \(\alpha\) and distinguish qualitatively different
behaviour according to \(|\alpha|\).

3.  Combination of techniques.  
   • For \(|\alpha|\le 1\) we have to map the problem to the classic one
     (substitution \(w=\alpha z\)).  
   • For \(|\alpha|>1\) we need a completely new ingredient:
     analysis of the monotone real function \(t\mapsto f_{n}(-t)\) and an
     explicit construction of a zero via (4); purely algebraic or
     triangle–inequality arguments no longer suffice.

4.  Sharpness argument.  Establishing that the boundary \(|\alpha|=1\) is
   precise forces us to build an explicit family of zeros and to verify
   that no further improvement is possible.

All of these additions make the enhanced variant
substantially more intricate than both the original Putnam problem and
the intermediate kernel version, while keeping the same core object
(the polynomial with descending coefficients).
